License-based gating with sap_licenses (schema version 3.23.0+): Use attribute: sap_licenses to gate scenarios based on which SAP product licenses a user holds.

Combining ias_attributes with ibn_targets (schema version 3.15.0+): Add a second object to the visibility_condition.objects array with type: ibn_targets to require both group membership and Work Zone navigation target availability. Since all objects in the array must evaluate to true, this creates an AND condition across the two types.

 

Conclusion

The visibility_condition field gives you a declarative, zero-code mechanism for controlling which users can access which scenarios in your Joule capability. The access control lives entirely in the scenario YAML. There's no conditional logic in your function code, no middleware to configure, and the only changes required are to your IAS configuration (for example, creating groups).

The key points to take away:

  • Scenarios with no visibility_condition are always visible to all end users
  • ias_attributes reads directly from the IAS identity token at runtime
  • match: ANY grants access if the user is in at least one listed group; match: ALL requires membership in every listed group

In the next post in this series, we'll look at document grounding: connecting Joule to enterprise document repositories so responses are grounded in your organization's actual content.

entry.title: Is this a sign SAP is losing the AI race?
entry.content:
Most of us are aware of SAP new API policy and its effects on external AI usage. Most of the policy is certainly about legitimate concerns on API usage, and it's effects on system stability. While I'm a strong believer in the customer's ability to manage his system as he wants, overuse of APIs with millions of request can be problematic and more and more it's SAP's system not the customer's.

But then we have this: "Any AI platform can connect to SAP through endorsed architectures and pathways" and then "Any AI platform can connect to SAP through endorsed architectures and pathways, such as A2A protocol via Joule and Agent Gateway", with a reference to the SAP Architecture Center which is all about BTP, Joule and A2A (so Joule).

In summary and in my view:

  • API policy on usage: Legitimate concern regarding stability, despite the fact i ultimately believe customer should be able to do what they want with their system.
  • Mandatory A2A with Joule: If there is overusage of APIs that is already covered by the general API policy, making this additional clause seem vendor lock-in and protectionism. I've proposed A2A architectures to customers, but it shouldn't be mandatory as a matter of principle.

When do companies resort to lock-in and protectionism? When they can't compete on the open market. We have tariffs on automobiles where Europe tariffs China because BYD is too cheap, and US tariffs the EU because EU doesn't want its cars.

So why are many people resorting to external agents instead of building on Joule? Three things SAP needs to solve, and fast:

  • SAP Development with AI: Claude Code does all my javascript and python code at scale and with quality, at this point i don't code, i do specs. Code outside of SAP ecosystem is orders of magnitude cheaper because AI is bad at doing SAP code. SAP MCP servers are just a patch, not a solution.
  • Joule Skills: Joule needs to do more. I need to have a reason to use Joule besides the fact it has SAP branding, and if Joule was able to do things like "create a payment specification for item A and B" as part of the LLM knowledge (or SAP built skills), it would have a real competitive advantage. But it doesn't, and it knows as much about SAP as the general LLM that it is using. 
  • Local AI: As tokens get more expensive customers will move to self hosted AI models that handle day to day tasks. We don't need Opus or GPT-5.5 for basic interpretation. SAP does have self hosting and large enterprises will want to go down that path, but mid-range customers just want to run their AI operations in house with the hardware they have.

From my perspective i'll continue to advocate for responsible API usage and scenarios that avoid massive data scraping, which is something your ERP shouldn't be subjected to. That's the real concern here and what we should be focusing, while SAP should be focusing on making Joule better instead of trying to make it mandatory. If stability is the concern it shouldn't matter if the usage is being generated by AI or a custom Node.js program.

At the end of the day S/4 HANA is an amazing product and customers need to be able to interact with it the way they want. Make Joule smarter about SAP and customers will choose to use it above other solutions.

entry.title: Joule AI Custom Solution for Material Shortage and Fulfillment
entry.content:
A few weeks ago, I had the opportunity to participate in a hackathon to build a Joule AI solution that addresses material shortages in an expedited manner. This can help Inventory Management users save time and money.

I am sure many SAP customers have similar requirements, and I want to share my experience here so it can be beneficial to others.

Problem Statement: IM users have to manually check shortages in SAP applications and create purchase requisitions if there is any shortage, to make sure production runs smoothly. Users perform this activity in the backend S/4HANA system.

Solution Architecture & Design: I built the artifacts below as part of this Joule project to address the requirement.

  1. Actions to get material shortages and create purchase requisitions.
  2. Skills to call these APIs and perform tasks.
  3. An agent that can process user requests.
  4. A test environment to deploy and test the project.

A destination to the backend system already exists to leverage the APIs and to create and test actions.

Actions built in SAP Build:

The action below was created to read material shortages from the backend by leveraging the Material Coverage API.

I also created a material input variable to filter the API using the material number. You can adjust the input and output as needed and test them in this section.

A condition is applied to map the user input material to the API material attribute in the C_MaterialCoverage entity.

Like the Material Coverage API, I configured another action to create a purchase requisition in the backend system. Please remember to enable CSRF protection (if applicable) for that POST API call.

Now let’s go into the Joule Studio to build the Joule project. As you can see from the screenshot below, a couple of Joule skills and an agent have been created.

This Joule skill is responsible for creating purchase requisition in the backend system.

Joule skills can also be consumed directly without an agent. You can enable or disable the functionality, as highlighted below.

These are the minimum inputs required to create a purchase requisition. Of course, it depends on how the backend is configured for a particular document type.

The purchase requisition number is the output from the skill.

Everything we’ve seen in the screenshots above is related to the Trigger part.

Below is the step where the corresponding Purchase Requisition action has been configured. A backend destination variable is also configured to create the purchase requisition in the corresponding backend system.

There are multiple other options available to use as well, like Process, Condition, etc.

The Trigger inputs are mapped to the Action inputs.

This is the output from the Action.

At the end the Action result is mapped to the Purchase Requisition variable which we are interested in.

Like the Purchase requisition skill, Material Shortage skill is also configured.

Now it’s time to configure the agent.

It’s important to set the expertise and instructions so the agent understands how to behave.

There are multiple LLM providers available in the Model Settings: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and Mistral AI.

You can select any provider and the LLM they offer for response generation and planning.

Once everything is done, release and deploy the application.

A new test environment has been created under Control Tower to deploy and test this project.

To launch the agent, navigate to the Joule tab and click Launch.

It’s testing time.

I entered a prompt to pull the shortage information for a product. As you can see in the screenshot below, the agent responded with the shortage details. This information is pulled from the backend system.

Next, I want to create a purchase requisition. The expectation was that the agent would reuse as much information as possible from the previous step and ask questions only if additional input was required.

As expected, the agent understood the request and figured out that some inputs were still missing to create the purchase requisition. It asked for those details to proceed.

Here you go. The requisition has been created in the backend.

You can check the log by clicking the highlighted button top right corner.

Conclusion: The Joule AI solution is flexible enough to understand user requests and process them. Users don't have to navigate through multiple screens to complete the job.

AI solutions are powerful because they make many processes simple and easy. They can support different scenarios depending on how the agent is configured. There are J4D, J4C, and other tools that can help developers, consultants, and businesses save a lot of time and add value.

Key Challenges: I found that date mapping is not easy in the formula editor, since the skill inputs are always strings.

Error handling is also challenging. The skill doesn’t provide a way to map action/API errors so the agent can guide the user to resolve the issue in the back end. For example: The Purchasing Organization doesn’t exist or authorization errors.

SAP Joule Studio is evolving, and I hope these gaps will be addressed soon.

Please feel free to comment if you have any questions. l will try to answer as much as I can.

Thank you!

entry.title: The Flyweel is turning: Partners leverage SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP BTP during Hack2Build
entry.content:
In the fast-paced digital landscape of 2025, SAP unveiled its plans for SAP Business Data Cloud as well as SAP HANA Cloud at SAP TechEd 2025, positioning itself as a leading force in data-driven innovation. Building on these announcements, SAP’s latest Hack2Build event explored the full potential of building applications on SAP Business Data Cloud.

The Hack2Build on Building Applications on SAP Business Data Cloud provided an immersive environment for partners to leverage the advanced capabilities of SAP Business Data Cloud as well as HANA Cloud and BTP, thus tapping even into the realm of agentic AI.  Equipped with hands-on coaching from SAP experts, participating partners became frontrunners in leveraging cutting-edge technologies, empowering them to transform insights into actionable solutions.

During the Hack2Build initiative, partners engaged in a seven-day hack phase, constructing functional applications that harnessed the power of real-time data analytics, advanced AI-driven business intelligence and actionable insights. The event emphasized seamless integration with SAP and non-SAP systems, and the capability to translate raw data into analytical insights and from there into strategic actions.

The culmination of the event featured a Live Demo Jam, where partners presented their innovative prototypes to a panel of SAP judges. This collaborative event showcased the ingenuity and potential of SAP's latest technology advancements, paving the way for future applications that bridge the gap between data insights and actionable business strategies.

It was again amazing to see how partners turned their innovative solution visions into running prototypes within seven days and delivering even full breakthroughs from sourcing the data through analytical modeling into leveraging the insights in Joule agents. The flywheel is truly turning now!

Participating Partners:

Participating Partners in the Hack2Build Building Applications on BDC, Apr 22-29 2026

🏆Winners

With that momentum in motion, the judges selected three solutions that stood out across technical completeness, workflow clarity, and customer relevance.

🥇NTT Data Business Solutions 

Manufacturers often face unexpected disruptions that reduce operational efficiency and can delay customer deliveries. The Intelligent Manufacturing solution addresses this by using SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Business Technology Platform to combine data from SAP ERP, SAP Data Products, custom Data Products, machine telemetry, and energy consumption. These data sources are used to train AI models in SAP Databricks, enabling early detection of production orders at risk due to supplier delays, plant failures, equipment issues, lack of resources, or other factors. The solution provides actionable insights through SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Build, while SAP Build Process Automation triggers real-time notifications for risk mitigation.

 

🥈cbs Consulting

cbs solution AI-Driven Dynamic Safety Stock Optimization aims at transforming supply chain resilience using SAP BDC, BTP, and AI. By integrating SAP S/4HANA and SAP IBP data with external market drivers (Oil prices, Forex Rates, & Trade Routes) via SAP Datasphere, the data is harmonized, while predictive models from SAP Databricks assess demand variability, supply risk, and external factors to generate optimal stock recommendations. SAP Analytics Cloud provides simulations, insights, and planning capabilities, while SAP Build Apps triggers the multi-level approval workflow and operationalise decisions back into core systems (SAP S/4HANA). The result? A closed-loop, intelligent process that reduces excess inventory and boosts market responsiveness.

 

🥉SDG Group

In oil and gas, an unplanned equipment failure costs $45,000–$60,000 per hour. The manual response cycle stretches that loss across 48 hours on average. Rig-Guard consolidates historical NPT records and live sensor readings (temperature, pressure, vibration) into a single source of truth on SAP BDC, then runs a predictive ML model on SAP Databricks to catch failures before they happen. When a prediction fires, an agentic workflow on SAP Build Process Automation takes it from insight to action: Autonomous above 95% confidence, Human in the Loop below - failure caught, revenue protected. The rig watches itself. The rig never stops.

 

⭐ Finalists

All other finalists proved themselves to be winners in their own right through the enthusiasm and creativity they demonstrated, underscoring the vitality of SAP's partner ecosystem. Across industries such as finance, supply chain, retail, and more, these accomplished partners delivered BDC-based solutions with clear business relevance and strong potential for rapid customer adoption. Their impressive prototypes showcased how Applications, Data & AI come to life and truly turn the flywheel.

 

Partner Company

Solution Title

All for One

AI Planning Insight Agent for SAP Analytics Cloud & Datasphere

cbs

AI-Driven Dynamic Safety Stock Optimization

Deloitte

CFO Impact Analyzer

DXC

SAP Customer Delivery Preference Identification

EY

MarketIQ

IBM

Revenue Timing & Value Conflicts with Tariff Resolution

Infosys

Infosys Size Curve Prediction Solution for Retail

KPMG

AI Copilot for Real-Time Supplier Risk & Sourcing Decisions

NTT DATA Business Solutions

Manufacturing Intelligence

PWC

24/7 Reconciliation & Monitoring

 

What’s Next

All participating partners now move into a six‑week Build Phase, where they will refine and extend their prototypes with support from SAP product management, Partner Ecosystem Success as well as many other experts in BDC, BTP and AI. This phase will focus on preparing solutions for customer validation and potential go‑to‑market discussions.

Acknowledgments

Many thanks to all partner teams, Judges, Databricks team and SAP contributors across Business Data Cloud Product Management, Customer Success & Delivery, Value Prototyping, BTP, BDC and AI Solution Architects, Partner Excellence Center Leads and Partner Ecosystem Success for making this edition of Hack2Build a milestone in spinning the flywheel of Applications, Data & AI!

entry.title: SAP Sapphire 2026 - Join me in our SAP BDC Sessions!
entry.content:
A couple of words about me: 

Hi, I'm Prakash! I've spent the last 30+ years in the enterprise software world, driven by one passion: turning messy, complex data into something teams can actually trust and act on. I founded Paxata to give customers a simpler, self-service way to prepare data at scale, and before that started Velosel focused on master data management. Most recently, I led DocuSign's Emerging Products & Technology group as Global VP and GM, where my team built the data fabric, AI, search, and analytics platform powering their applications. Today, I have the incredible privilege of leading SAP Business Data Cloud and I can tell you, this is the most exciting chapter yet. We are on a quest to make SAP BDC the enterprise data platform of choice for the agentic era, and we are just getting started!!

I am thrilled to share with you my sessions at SAP Sapphire Orlando 2026. I would love to see you there!

Accelerate insights across your organization with SAP Business Data Cloud | BDC2061
This one is close to my heart. We'll dive into how SAP Business Data Cloud brings SAP data and intelligence together: curated metrics, built-in AI models, and planning capabilities that you can actually put to work today. The focus: accelerating real, high-impact business scenarios like improving working capital or combining workforce insights across SAP and non-SAP sources. This is the power of contextual, business-ready data in action.

Getting to know data products in SAP Business Data Cloud | BDC2747
The fastest path to value? Start with data products that already "know" your business context. In this session, we'll cover what SAP data products are, how they fit into your landscape, and why they matter for both business and IT leaders. If you want to see how SAP Business Data Cloud delivers trusted, AI-ready data at scale—this is the session for you!

Related community managed tags you should subscribe to remain informed about this topic: 
SAP Business Data Cloud

Related community topic pages: 
SAP Business Data Cloud

Looking forward to seeing you: 
If you're at Sapphire Orlando, I would love for you to join me and the team. Across these sessions, we'll get deep into how SAP Business Data Cloud helps you bring together SAP and non-SAP data, work with curated business-ready data products, and put AI and planning capabilities to use for real scenarios - from faster insights to better decisions. Deep gratitude to our incredible customers, partners, and SAP colleagues who make this journey possible. See you in Orlando!!!

entry.title: Manage Quality Certificate and SAP Document AI
entry.content:
Hello SAP community! Today I would like to share a new feature to address a big common pain for a lot of customers related to managing quality certificates.

Handling incoming supplier certificates is often a manual task. These documents typically arrive separately via email and are stored as attachments on your desktop, sometimes in different formats. If necessary, they may be filed manually, requiring additional time to compare results and align data with quality inspections. This process is time-consuming and makes tracking difficult.

To address these challenges, the development team aims to minimize delays and reduce oversight of documents. This will allow us to focus on more important activities. With the release of 2025 version, a new Fiori application for managing incoming quality certificates is now available!

The new app is named Manage Certificate Receipts, which allows you to centralize this process, providing users a chance to record certificates, track their status, and view details all in one single place. The app is directly linked to goods receipt, allowing us to cleanly assign certificates to purchase orders. The main objective is to serve a management tool to monitor the receipt of quality certificates from suppliers, ensuring that each required certificate is received for the respective materials and stored in DMS. You can define the necessity of these certificates in the material master and track their receipt independently of the goods receipt through various methods, including manual recording and automatic system updates.

As you can see in the figure, the app displays the list of Certificates based of filter criteria such as certificate type, status, material, purchasing document, etc...  From the list, you can deep dive into the object page for each one to view the certificate receipts details

From the worklist you can also create a manual certificate receipts, which is a feature to enable the creation via API (Quality Certificate Receipt - Create, Update (A2X)), by the system or just manually for example for those cases when you are receiving the certificate even before the GR is posted. You can find more information here: Creating Certificate Receipts

The effect is greater transparency and less manual work.!!

But the magic really happens when If SAP Document AI is integrated in S/4HANA Public Cloud that will enable a new option of 'start certificate upload' to directly navigate to the Upload Quality Certificate Documents app, to upload certificates and see the full list of all the uploaded documents from there... With this functionality you will use the AI-assisted processing of incoming quality certificates and accelerate the confirmation of certificates and speeding up the entire process of inspection and stock release. All you have to do is to upload the document from your desk, and AI will take care of matching the information; the system support several formats such as PDF  and JPEG!!

As you can see in the figure above, the document has different statuses, after uploading, first you will note the In progress status, meaning that the system is processing and reading the documentation and extract all the data in the background, once ready it will change to Ready for Posting and immediately after to confirmed, meaning that at this point of time, you can see the certificate link generated to display the object page and to review the information uploaded and administrative data relevant. Besides and most importantly, the system creates a certificate matched where you will be able to review the collected data on the background,  validate the details of log messages and make adjustments or solve inconsistencies in case needed. On the figure below you see how is possible to compare the information from the document uploaded vs the data collected after matching the information to approve, edit or complete the match:

If you are interest of knowing more about this app and the new AI features, please check the following links:

Manage Certificate Receipts - SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library

Quality Certificates for Goods Receipt | SAP Help Portal

Manage Certificate Receipts | SAP Help Portal

Integration of SAP Document AI | SAP Help Portal

SAP Discovery Center AI Feature - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, processing of incoming quality certificates with SAP Document AI

Note: The integration of uploading a quality certificate document using SAP Document AI, is currently planned in the QM roadmap to be released by Q3/Q4 of 2026 for Private cloud edition as well, meantime remains available only for public cloud...

Hope you find the information relevant for your business need!

Enjoy!

entry.title: How SAP Teams Are Finding T-Codes Now
entry.content:
If you've supported SAP users for any length of time, you've answered some version of this question more times than you'd like: "What's the T-code to do X again?"

It comes from new joiners, from finance people who occasionally need an MM transaction, from supply chain folks who dip into WM. And from consultants too, when we step outside our home module. SAP's transaction-code system spans tens of thousands of codes across MM, SD, FI, CO, PP, HR/HCM, PM, QM, WM, PS, Basis, ABAP, CRM, SRM, BW, and the rest. Nobody memorizes all of it, and nobody should have to.

What's changed over the last couple of years is the way teams answer that question. Not because anyone has gotten better at memorizing, but because the tooling around T-code discovery has improved. I want to share what I've seen working on a few recent projects, and what hasn't.

Why the old approaches stopped scaling

Most teams cycle through the same lookup methods, roughly in the order they adopt them.

Printed cheat sheets and PDFs. Every SAP project I've joined had one floating around. They're useful on day one and stale by month three. The moment SAP renames a transaction, splits one in two, or marks something as "do not use in S/4HANA," the cheat sheet starts misleading people.

The SAP Easy Access menu and Favorites. Complete in theory, unbrowsable in practice for anyone who doesn't already know the menu path. I've watched experienced users scroll for two minutes looking for something they could have typed in four characters, if only they remembered which four.

SE93 (Maintain Transaction Codes). Useful when you know part of the code or want to inspect the underlying program. Less useful when all you know is what the business process is called in plain English.

Internal wikis and Confluence pages. These work right up until the person who curated them leaves, or until S/4HANA introduces Fiori-app equivalents that nobody backfilled into the wiki.

Generic web search. Returns an ocean of forum posts of varying ages, half of them referencing transactions that have been deprecated or replaced. You usually find the answer. It just takes a lot of clicking for a four-character payoff.

What all of these methods share is an unspoken assumption: that the user already knows enough SAP vocabulary to translate intent into transaction code. For cross-functional users and new joiners, that assumption is exactly where the wheels come off.

What's different now

Two things changed recently that make the problem more tractable.

The first is that the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library has gotten noticeably better. SAP recently relaunched it at fal.cloud.sap (the older URL fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com still works for now). You can search by classic T-code and see the corresponding Fiori app or apps, the business role, the line of business, and the product version. For S/4HANA migration work, this has saved me a lot of guessing. It's the authoritative SAP source for the ECC-to-Fiori mapping question.

The second is that AI-assisted search has reached the point where natural-language queries actually work. Vector embeddings let a phrase like "create a purchase order" land in the same semantic neighborhood as ME21N, even though the two share no keywords. LLM re-ranking then surfaces the most likely match at the top of the list with a short explanation. Together, those two pieces produce a search experience that works for people who don't already speak SAP.

I've tried a few community-built tools in this space. One I keep coming back to is TCodeAI (tcodeai.vercel.app), a free web app that lets you search either by code (MIRO) or by intent ("post a vendor invoice") and ranks the results with a short note on why each match was suggested. It also includes Fiori-app mappings alongside the classic codes, which helps during the dual-stack ECC/S/4HANA period most teams are living through. I'm not affiliated with it. I find it a useful complement to the official Fiori Apps Library when I want a faster, conversational lookup.

Other tools are moving in the same direction, including SAP's own Joule copilot work and various module-specific search add-ons. I expect the space to keep maturing.

A few caveats are worth keeping in mind for any AI-assisted T-code tool:

  • Always verify against your own system before sharing a code with end users. What's available depends on your release, your activated business functions, and your authorizations.
  • Treat AI explanations as starting points, not gospel. These tools are good at narrowing the field, less good at understanding your specific configuration.
  • Deprecated and S/4HANA-restricted transactions are a moving target. Cross-check anything critical against the SAP S/4HANA Simplification List or the Simplification Item Catalog in the SAP ONE Support Portal.

A practical workflow for 2026

If I had to describe what a healthy T-code lookup workflow looks like today, it would be roughly this:

  1. For Fiori and S/4HANA mapping questions, start with the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library.
  2. For "I know what I want to do but not what it's called," use whatever AI-assisted search tool fits your team's comfort level. TCodeAI is one option, but it isn't the only one.
  3. For deep validation, including release behavior, deprecation status, and replacement guidance, use the SAP S/4HANA Simplification Item Catalog and the linked SAP Notes via the SAP ONE Support Portal.
  4. Keep your team's internal cheat sheet small. Focus it on the codes specific to your customizations and Z-transactions.

The broader point is this. We've spent a long time treating T-code memorization as a kind of rite of passage in SAP. It doesn't have to be. The tooling has caught up enough that new joiners can focus on understanding the process and look up the codes the way the rest of us look up everything else.

I'd be curious to hear what others are using. If you've landed on a workflow that's cut down on "what's the T-code for...?" interruptions in your team, leave a note in the comments.

entry.title: SAP Sapphire Orlando 2026: Unlock AI agent value with SAP Signavio solutions
entry.content:
Hello everyone, I am Noor Zehra Naqvi (many people call me Noor),  a Senior Director of Product Management at SAP Signavio. My focus area is where AI, data, and analytics meet.

Today I would like to introduce my SAP Sapphire session:

Unlock AI agent value with SAP Signavio solutions | BTM1573

In the session I’ll share our strategy about AI Agent Excellence: how to discover, govern, maintain, and continuously improve agents so they deliver real value in business processes.

AI agents are becoming a new reality. Companies are increasing their investments, and we’re seeing more and more agents show up as part of the workforce, not only as a concept, but in real operational workflows.

From there, the key question becomes: where should you apply agents, and how do you scale them responsibly? This is where SAP Signavio helps by giving agents the right process and business context, and by supporting the guardrails organizations need so agents remain compliant, predictable, and aligned with how the business actually runs.

We’ll also dive into governance and transparency, how you can understand the impact of agents on your end-to-end processes, and how to validate conformance. For example, through what we call agent mining, you can see where agents touch a process, what variants they introduce, and whether those variants are conforming or not. This level of visibility is essential when you move from a few pilots to broad adoption.

To keep it practical, one of our customers, Albatha Group will join us to share how SAP Signavio supported their AI and AI Agents journey and helped create value in their organization. We’ll close with a sneak peek at our latest releases on Agentic AI.

Interested in learning more?

 

My biggest recommendation is: get started and experiment. Try out AI agents, see what’s possible, and quite literally get your hands dirty. At the same time, scaling agents inside an organization requires a framework: strong security, clear control mechanisms, and governance that prevents unexpected or unwanted behavior.

That’s why it’s worth investing time in understanding how SAP Signavio supports this journey, so you’re set up not just to build agents, but to operate them with confidence.

Also, beyond my own session), I’d encourage you to explore a few other Signavio-related sessions at SAP Sapphire, especially those that help you connect strategy with execution:

  • Unlocking the new frontier of agentic transformation with SAP Signavio | BTM2176
  • Deliver measurable transformation value confidently with SAP Signavio | BTM1574
  • Build a transformation capability that delivers measurable value | BTM1286
  • Crafting the future: The SAP Signavio solutions 2026 road map | BTM1715

 

Now let’s hear from you!

 

In the comments’ section, it would be great to hear where you are on your agentic AI journey:

  • How far along are you, are you still piloting, or already scaling?
  • Roughly how many agents do you have running today?
  • What are the biggest struggles, blockers and risks you’re seeing as you scale?

 

If you’re attending SAP Sapphire, I hope you’ll join the conversation, both during the event and in the comments. The shift to agentic AI is exciting, but the organizations that win will be the ones that pair innovation with strong process context, governance, and transparency.

entry.title: How to Set Up a Python Virtual Environment
entry.content:
Introduction:

A Python virtual environment is a local directory that maintains project dependencies distinct from the rest of the system. It prevents conflicts of version and provides a uniform development in various presuppositions. This is needed to create clean Python applications that are maintainable and scalable.

 

Open Visual studio and create a folder { To learn on How to step up Visual Studio for python programming , please refer my blog https://community.sap.com/t5/artificial-intelligence-blogs-posts/ai-steps-to-setup-python-programming/ba-p/14381631 }

 

 

Create Virtual environment using python -m venv {environment name}

 

 

 

Create folder for database , ui, and text file for requirements/dependencies

 

 

Add dependencies in requirement file

 

 

Install dependencies using command  

pip install -r {requirement text file name}

 

 

 

Activate Virtual environment

 

 

 

Summary 

A python virtual environment helps to isolate project dependencies and prevents conflicts with the rest of the system. It provides uniform, hygienic and controllable growth in various setups.

 

##If you found this blog helpful, please consider giving it a lik

entry.title: SAP Joule- The "Totally not an official ITSM connector"
entry.content:
SAP Joule isn’t a magical ITSM plug that sits between systems wearing a badge. It’s more like an enthusiastic translator at a networking event who says, “Oh, you’ve got an incident in ServiceNow? I’ll just whisper that into the API for you.”

So instead of “SAP Joule is an ITSM connector” Think: “SAP Joule intelligent assistant is that clever middle layer in SAP applications like S4HANA, Build Work Zone/Fiori that chats with ServiceNow via APIs, turns your casual complaints into structured incidents, and then politely updates them when new test results arrive—like a very overqualified messenger who never complains about copy-pasting JSON and knows how to get the job done through APIs while you just stand there wondering how it became so competent..”

In short: it’s not officially an ITSM connector , but it fakes it quite efficiently, powered entirely by APIs and good intentions. ( Using SAP Joule Studio in Build and custom Joule skills)

Incident creation from SAP Joule

When users interact with SAP Build Work Zone/Fiori or any SAP Joule enabled application, SAP Joule can act as an embedded AI assistant that interprets user requests, system alerts, or test outcomes. Instead of switching tools, users can simply describe an issue in natural language (for example, “login failure in payroll app after deployment or system dump”), and SAP Joule can:

 

  • Format the problem into a structured ITSM ticket
  • Create a new incident directly in ServiceNow

 

This reduces friction between business users and IT operations teams, ensuring incidents are logged consistently and quickly and with improved quality.

As shown below, SAP Joule asks to provide some mandatory inputs ( can be customized), which can be ingested in one single well drafted prompt, separated by commas and an incident is submitted in service now in Realtime.

Incident successfully created with the right inputs. Some inputs can be made mandatory to control the incident quality and later operational analysis.

Updating incidents with testing results

Beyond creation, SAP Joule can also manage the lifecycle of incidents. As users validate fixes or reproduce issues, they can feed results back into SAP Joule, which then:

  • Updates existing ServiceNow incidents with comments
  • Reflects status changes such as “In Testing,” “Resolved,” or “Reopened” (possible)

For example, after testing, a user can simply instruct SAP Joule to append results to an open incident. The update is immediately reflected in ServiceNow, ensuring real-time synchronization between SAP and ITSM systems.

Advantages of using SAP Joule for incident generation

Integrating SAP Joule with ServiceNow could provide several operational benefits:

  • Faster incident creation: Users can log incidents directly from business workflows without switching platforms
  • Reduced manual effort: Natural language processing eliminates the need for structured form filling
  • Improved accuracy: Context-aware AI reduces incomplete or inconsistent incident descriptions.
  • Seamless integration: Direct connection between SAP applications and ServiceNow improves end-to-end visibility
  • Real-time updates: Test results and comments can be pushed instantly into active incident

Summary

I only discussed two possibilities but the capabilities could go far beyond this.

  • Search incidents or Search known solutions
  • Read knowledge articles in service now (Document grounding with Service Now)
  • Auto assign Category/sub-category based on inputs or past incidents.

By positioning SAP Joule as an intelligent ITSM companion, organizations could bridge SAP-centric business processes with ServiceNow-based incident management. SAP Joule Embedded within SAP applications, could transform how incidents are created, enriched, and resolved—making IT operations more responsive, automated, and closely aligned with business activity.

Note:  GenAI was used for language refinement and tone improvement.

entry.title: ABAP AI: Creating Custom Generative AI Scenarios in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Artificial Intelligence in ABAP – Ready for Today

Artificial Intelligence capabilities are no longer limited to external services or side-by-side extensions. With recent SAP S/4HANA releases, AI scenarios can be consumed and modeled directly within ABAP, allowing existing applications and processes to be enhanced with Generative AI in an enterprise-ready way.

At the center of this enablement is the ABAP AI SDK powered by Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management (ISLM). It provides the runtime APIs and lifecycle concepts required to define, manage, and consume AI scenarios from ABAP code in a consistent manner. This makes it possible for customers and partners to build Custom AI scenarios inside their own SAP S/4HANA systems, closely aligned with business logic and system architecture.

Why ABAP AI SDK and ISLM Matter

The ABAP AI SDK powered by ISLM, enables ABAP developers to interact with Generative AI models without embedding provider-specific logic into applications. ISLM acts as the central framework and lifecycle component for AI scenarios, while the ABAP AI SDK exposes standardized APIs to consume these scenarios at runtime.

Beyond pure large language model (LLM) interactions, SAP AI Core provides an orchestration service. Orchestration in SAP AI Core is a managed service that enables unified access, control, and execution of generative AI models through standardized APIs, prompt templating, and configurable AI workflow components.

Using orchestration, LLM interactions can be enriched with additional capabilities such as data masking, content filtering, document grounding, and translation. The ABAP AI SDK powered by ISLM in ABAP can leverage both direct LLM interactions and orchestration features, allowing ABAP applications to consume advanced AI workflows while keeping governance and lifecycle management aligned with SAP standards. 

These capabilities are available as of SAP S/4 HANA 2023.

Learn by Doing: An SAP Developer Mission

To provide a structured, hands-on entry point, we offer an SAP Developer Mission that guides you through the setup and usage of Custom AI scenarios in ABAP.

The mission consists of a tutorial group, guiding you how to set up your SAP S/4 HANA system for custom AI scenarios and dedicated tutorials demonstrating how Custom GenAI scenarios are built and consumed from ABAP using the ABAP AI SDK, including orchestration-based interactions with generative models.

Who Is This Relevant For?

The mission is intended for:

  • ABAP developers who want to understand how Generative AI can be consumed from ABAP
  • Architects evaluating AI integration options for SAP S/4HANA
  • Development teams exploring Custom AI scenarios within their existing SAP landscapes

No prior AI expertise is required, but familiarity with ABAP development is assumed.

Getting Started

Ready? Let's explore the SAP Developer Mission 

👉Get Started with GenAI Development in SAP S/4HANA 

which provides a guided and practical introduction on  Generative AI scenario creation and consumption in ABAP.

Want to Learn More?

  • SAP Help Portal: ABAP AI & ISLM Documentation
  • SAP Community Page: Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management for SAP S/4HANA 
  • SAP Community Blog: Scaling Generative AI in SAP S/4HANA Enterprise-Ready Innovations with ISLM 
  • SAP Community Blog: Developing AI‑enabled applications with the ABAP AI SDK (Public Cloud & Steampunk)

entry.title: PORTAL - Understanding the Need for Secure & Responsible AI at SAP
entry.content:
In one of the most crucial sectors – Aviation, safety relies on identifying and mitigating risks through robust, multi-layered systems designed to counter cognitive biases and organizational failures. Modern safety management integrates human factors, data-driven analytics, and rigorous testing to improve reliability and trust. Aircrafts need to go through thorough inspection and testing to prove their airworthiness before they are permitted to operate. Similarly, SAP’s Global AI Ethics Policy emphasizes principles such as fairness, privacy, robustness, transparency, and security. When SAP develops its own AI capabilities, these principles guide every phase—from dataset curation to deployment, with model benchmarking and evaluation being a critical part to ensure AI safety and security (see the keep_the_blue_side_up blogpost series).

One area of growing importance is SAP’s work with in-house foundation models. While many assume SAP only integrates external LLMs, the reality is different: SAP also builds its own foundational models tailored to business data, enterprise processes, and multi-modal use cases. One of these is the research work PORTAL.

For foundation models, it is important to ensure they are resilient and secure. Our recent evaluation of the research work PORTAL focuses exactly on this: understanding how PORTAL behaves when subjected to adversarial stress tests, privacy attacks, distribution shifts, and robustness checks. 
These findings are summarized here in a transparent and accessible way, based on the detailed technical analysis conducted internally.

Why Security and Robustness Testing Is Critical for Foundation Models

Foundation models learn from vast quantities of structured (tabular) and sometimes unstructured data. PORTAL is trained on substantial amount of tabular data. This gives foundation models impressive generalization capabilities but also exposes them to potential vulnerabilities.

To ensure that PORTAL meets SAP’s enterprise requirements, we evaluated it against a curated subset of the OWASP Top 10 for Machine Learning, focusing on attacks that are relevant to proprietary foundation models

 What Is PORTAL? 

PORTAL is an SAP research work for table-native foundation models. It:

  • learns from diverse, business-specific tabular datasets
  • supports multiple downstream tasks
  • can be fine-tuned for various enterprise scenarios
  • follows SAP’s policy-aligned AI safeguards by design

The following section presents the experiments conducted on PORTAL to assess the applicability of relevant OWASP threats within enterprise-focused environments.

Experiment : Sequential Fine-Tuning — Does PORTAL Retain Knowledge Safely?

We began by testing whether PORTAL can retain information across sequential training without leaking old data or overfitting to new data. We performed tests using the Breast Cancer dataset, which was available readily & had nearly equal distribution across various features & labels.

Case 1 — Extreme Distribution Shift (Label Flip)

Flipping class labels produced a dramatic drop in accuracy:

  • Before fine-tuning: 94.74%
  • After fine-tuning on flipped data: 8.07%

Interpretation: 
The model exhibited catastrophic forgetting, in that performance on the earlier fine-tuned task degraded substantially after adaptation to a strongly shifted distribution (label flip). This suggests that the model did not retain information from the previous fine-tuned dataset once trained on the new task, indicating overwriting of task-specific patterns during sequential fine-tuning. We emphasize that this observation is limited to the fine-tuning stage and does not provide evidence regarding memorization during pre-training.

Case 2 — Mild Feature Perturbations

We applied small additive and multiplicative Gaussian noise to simulate mild distribution shifts and fine-tuned the model on the perturbed data.

  • Before fine-tuning: 94.74%
  • After perturbation fine-tuning: 41.05%

Interpretation: 
PORTAL adapts better to controlled shifts. 
This demonstrates better generalization while still maintaining partial retention — the desirable behavior for enterprise adaptation, where gradual data drift is common. 

.      

Figure 1: Left: Case 1; Right: Case 2

Experiment : Membership Inference Attacks — Does PORTAL Leak Training Data? 
To evaluate privacy resilience, we analyzed whether PORTAL assigns systematically higher confidence scores to samples seen during recent fine-tuning compared to held-out samples. This experiment focuses exclusively on detecting potential membership signals in fine-tuning data and does not target memorization from pre-training.

Two datasets were used during fine-tuning:

  • 569-sample Breast Cancer dataset
  • 1000-sample augmented dataset (over-sampling)

For both settings, we compared the distributions of true-class confidence scores for member (train) and non-member (test/held out data) samples.

Findings: 

  • Smaller dataset → Larger confidence gap → Potentially higher privacy risk: In the smaller dataset setting, a mild shift in confidence distributions is observed, consistent with limited overfitting.
  • Larger dataset → Reduced confidence gap → Better privacy protection: In the augmented dataset setting, member and non-member confidence distributions exhibit strong overlap, indicating the absence of clear confidence-based separability.

 

 

 

 

 

 

Importantly, in neither setting does a reliable confidence threshold emerge that would enable effective membership inference. The increased overlap in the larger dataset further reduces residual leakage signals.

These results suggest that PORTAL exhibits low practical vulnerability to confidence-based membership inference attacks, and that increased data diversity and scale further enhance privacy robustness.  We emphasize that extracting memorized information from pre-training is substantially more challenging and remains outside the scope of this study.

Experiment : Knife-Edge Analysis — Understanding Feature Sensitivity

Knife-edge analysis helps identify features where very small changes can sharply flip the model’s prediction. This reveals whether the model is relying on balanced information or leaning too heavily on a single dominant signal. Two complementary tests were conducted:

  1. Global Sensitivity Test (Feature Shuffling / Permutation)
  2. Local Knife-Edge Test (Single Instance Feature Increment)

Comparative evaluation was performed on: PORTAL, Decision Tree, XGBoost

Case 1 -  Breast Cancer Dataset (Comparative Analysis) 

(The task is binary classification where, 0 = Malignant, 1 = Benign) 

Feature Importance Analysis: Before conducting knife-edge tests, we examined which features each model relied on most.

  • Portal: Mean Radius and Mean Perimeter, more weightage on Mean Radius
  • Decision Tree: Worst Radius has highest feature relevance
  • XGBoost: Worst Perimeter has the highest feature relevance 

The knife-edge experiments were then performed on the highest-ranked feature for each model.

.          . 

      PORTAL                                            Decision Tree                                                XGBoost

Global Sensitivity Test (Permutation): 

We shuffled the dominant feature for each model and measured accuracy change.

Run 1: 

       

PORTAL                                              Decision Tree                                                      XGBoost

Feature shuffled per model: PORTAL (Mean Radius), Decision Tree (Worst Radius), XGBoost (Worst Perimeter)

Run 

PORTAL Baseline (%) (Training) 

PORTAL Drop (%)(Testing) 

DT Baseline (%)(Training) 

DT Drop (%)(Testing) 

XGB Baseline (%)(Training) 

XGB Drop (%)(Testing) 

Run 1 

90.35 

13.16 

91.23 

7.02 

94.74 

−0.88 

Run 2 

87.72 

16.67 

91.23 

5.26 

94.74 

−0.88 

Run 3 

92.98 

17.54 

91.23 

3.51 

94.74 

−2.63 

Overall, the findings suggest that PORTAL relies more heavily on a single dominant feature, whereas tree-based models achieve greater robustness through distributed feature utilization.

Local Knife-Edge Test (Single Instance Analysis)

We selected a correctly predicted benign sample row:

  • mean radius = 11.13 (considered for PORTAL)
  • worst radius = 11.68 (considered for Decision Tree)
  • worst perimeter = 74.35 (considered for XGBoost)
  • true label = 1

We gradually increased the dominant feature (per model) from its original value in the selected sample up to the maximum observed value for that feature, while keeping all other features constant.

Observations:

     

PORTAL                                              Decision Tree                                             XGBoost

Comparative Sensitivity Summary:

Model 

Dominant Feature 

Global Accuracy Drop 

Local Flip Detected 

PORTAL  

Mean Radius 

High (−18.42%) 

Yes (at 16.03) 

Decision Tree 

Worst Radius 

Moderate (−7.02%) 

No 

XGBoost 

Worst Perimeter 

Minimal (+0.88%) 

No 

The results suggest that PORTAL places stronger emphasis on a single dominant scalar feature, whereas tree-based models (Decision Tree and XGBoost), despite assigning high importance to certain features, leverage a more distributed set of inputs during prediction.

Case 2 - Diabetes Dataset (PORTAL Corroboration Study)

The pattern repeated in the Diabetes dataset. 
When gradually changing Age, the prediction flipped from Diabetic → Non-diabetic at Age = 21, while adjusting Pregnancies had no impact. Again, one dominant feature drove the prediction.

    

Case 3 - Column Reordering

We also tested whether feature ordering affected predictions. Before reordering, occasional flips occurred during perturbations. After reordering, flips disappeared within the tested range — indicating that even column order influenced inference behaviour. 

    

For tree-based models (Decision Tree and XGBoost), column reordering was not applicable. These models bind feature indices during training and enforce strict feature-name alignment at inference time. As a result, predictions remain invariant to column order, and any mismatch in ordering typically raises a validation error rather than altering model output.

Overall Observation

Knife-edge evaluation across datasets shows that PORTAL tends to anchor its decisions around a few dominant scalar features. This can make the model efficient but also makes it more sensitive to noise and small perturbations. These insights help guide improvements in regularization and encourage more distributed feature learning, strengthening the model's robustness for real-world enterprise scenarios.

Experiment : Memorization Testing — Distinguishing True Learning from Learned Shortcuts

This experiment was conducted on the publicly available tutorial dataset containing multiple modality-based categories. We reduced the proportion of “audio” samples in the training set from 40% to 10% while increasing their share in the test set, forcing the model to classify predominantly unseen audio data. This means that, in the final case, the model was asked to classify 90% audio samples it had never seen before. Prior to splitting, explicit duplicate removal was performed between training and test sets to mitigate trivial data leakage.

Unexpected Outcome: Across every scenario, including when the model had seen only a small fraction of a category, the accuracy remained exceptionally high (99–100%). At first glance, this could be mistaken for excellent generalization. However, such results usually indicate the opposite.

Why This Matters

A model that performs perfectly on data it has never been trained on is often relying on:

  • memorized patterns from the initial training samples,
  • dataset artifacts, or
  • shortcut cues that do not represent true category understanding.

Why This Insight Is Important

If a model relies on memorized cues rather than meaningful learning, its performance could degrade significantly when exposed to real-world enterprise data, where such shortcuts may not exist. By identifying memorization tendencies early, we can:

  • Expand dataset diversity
  • Strengthen model robustness
  • Improve generalization across unseen business scenarios

Conclusion:

This evaluation helped us understand how PORTAL behaves under different security, robustness, and privacy conditions. Each experiment revealed meaningful insights - from how the model adapts to distribution shifts, to how it handles sensitive features, to where shortcut learning or memorization may appear. These findings directly support SAP’s goal of strengthening responsible, secure, and reliable foundation models for enterprise use. Analogically ensuring the flight is smooth, safe and reliable. PORTAL is another example of how AI transparency, robustness, and responsible evaluation help “keep the blue side up”.                                              

References:

  1. https://arxiv.org/pdf/2402.12715
  2. https://github.com/SAP-samples/data-attribute-recommendation-postman-tutorial/blob/main/Tutorial_Example_Dataset.csv
  3. https://help.sap.com/docs/data-attribute-recommendation
  4. https://github.com/SAP-samples/data-attribute-recommendation-postman-tutorial
  5. https://arxiv.org/pdf/2409.01380
